texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To develop an energy-efficient and available carbon dioxide (CO2) capture technique with amine-based solvents and membrane contactors (MCs), an innovative technological route was proposed to remove CO2 from industrial flue  gas by coupling MCs with catalyst-aided solvent regeneration (MC-CR). A laboratory-scale experimental setup with ceramic membrane module was then designed and built to accurately estimate the performance of the MC-CR route, using aqueous monoethanolamine (MEA) and powder metatitanic acid [TiO(OH)2] as the solvent and catalyst, respectively. The results indicated that the nanofluid effect of the catalyst reduced the mass transfer resistance and promoted a concentration change in the liquid film, resulting in a high CO2  absorption rate.The surface  acidic  sites  of  TiO(OH)2 boosted  the  process  of  proton  transfer  in  the  solvent,  thus  facilitating  the decomposition of carbamate. The CO2  capture efficiency increased by 9.48% and the energy consumption of solvent  regeneration  considerably  decreased  by  40.8%  at  dosage  of  2  wt%  TiO(OH)2.  In  addition,  matched pressure regulation for gas -liquid flow in the MCs effectively avoided membrane fouling and wetting after 180 min continuous operation. Industrial-scale simulation indicated that the MC-CR route achieved a low capture cost at $46.7/t CO2, a decrease of 28% compared with the cost of the traditional route. This study established the groundwork for developing MC technology as an effective approach for low-cost industrial CO2 capture.

A laboratory-scale experimental setup with ceramic membrane module was then designed and built to accurately estimate the performance of the MC-CR route, using aqueous monoethanolamine (MEA) and powder metatitanic acid [TiO(OH)2] as the solvent and catalyst, respectively. The results indicated that the nanofluid effect of the catalyst reduced the mass transfer resistance and promoted a concentration change in the liquid film, resulting in a high CO2  absorption rate.The surface  acidic  sites  of  TiO(OH)2 boosted  the  process  of  proton  transfer  in  the  solvent,  thus  facilitating  the decomposition of carbamate. The superior mass transfer efficiency of MC makes them highly advantageous for capturing carbon from flue gas with low-partial-pressure CO2  (less than 20 vol%) [7]. In this scenario, the membrane serves as a nonselective interfacial barrier between the liquid and gas phases, thereby preventing the volatilization and oxidative degradation of amine-based solvents during direct gas -liquid countercurrent contact [8]. However, achieving long-term stable absorption and efficient desorption for CO2 capture from industrial flue gas by using MC remains a serious challenge,  particularly  for  reducing  the  amount  of  energy  required  for amine-based solvent regeneration [9].

texts.orig: for  MC  with  amine  solvents.  However,  efficient  solvent  regeneration (carbamate decomposition) requires high temperatures, typically above 120 ◦ C  [10,11].  Therefore,  solvent  regeneration  remains  a  primary cause of the large energy penalty observed, mainly ascribed to the high sensible and latent heat consumption of water [12,13]. For pressurized flue  gas,  regeneration  below  the  boiling  point  of  the  solvent  can  be achieved  by  applying  a  sweep  gas  or  reduced  pressure  with  the  MC stripper, which considerably reduces energy demand [14,15]. However, this method is not suitable for nonpressurized industrial flue gas, and its practicality for commercial application on a large scale remains to be validated. Recently, catalyst-aided solvent regeneration has emerged as a promising approach for reducing the energy penalty [16]. According to previous studies, inorganic materials can be used as catalysts in the CO2  capture process. In addition, some mesoporous materials, such as H-ZSM [17,18], Al2O3  [19], COF [20], and MOF [21], and their derivatives  can  be  used  to  catalytically  accelerate  amine-based  solvent regeneration, thereby effectively reducing regeneration heat duty. The Lewis and Br ø nsted acid activity sites (LASs and BASs) of catalysts boost the processes of dominating proton transfer and C -N bond (carbamate) breakage with carbamate decomposition rate [22]. Once a rich solvent regenerates  at  a  relatively  low  temperature  with  high  desorption  kinetics, lower-grade heat resources can be used as a green approach for solvent regeneration. Metatitanic acid [TiO(OH)2], as a stable and cheap mineral acid, is a promising candidate for effectively stripping CO2 from spent monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent. Powder TiO(OH)2 dispersed in amine-based solvent flow is a straightforward technique to match of MC carbon  capture  systems.  These  microsized  fine  particles  enhance  the mass and heat transfer process between the gas and liquid phases and exert  a  nanofluid  effect,  which  accelerates  the  absorption  of  CO2. However,  the  effect  of  nanoscale  catalytic  particles  on  MCs  remains unclear, especially in the context of membrane wetting and pore plugging, thereby posing a critical problem for practical application.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ceramic membranes have been used by many researchers in membrane  absorption  because  of  their  acid  and  base  stability,  corrosion resistance, high temperature  resistance,  and  mechanical  strength [23 -25]. However, the hydrophilicity of the surface hydroxyl groups (OH ) results in membrane wetting, and even a small number of wetting pores may substantially increase the total mass transfer resistance, according to previous reports [26,27]. Compared with surface hydrophobic modification, regulation of the operating conditions of MC is a more economical and feasible method to avoid membrane wetting [28 -30]. In practice, the membrane state (liquid wetting and gas bubbles) can be predicted using an individual pore state model, which is mainly related to pore size and operation pressure [31]. However, how to adjust the pressure difference between gas-liquid phase is still a challenge to efficiently avoid membrane wetting, bubbling and plugging in presence of catalytic particles.
texts.text: Ceramic membranes have been used by many researchers in membrane  absorption  because  of  their  acid  and  base  stability,  corrosion resistance, high temperature  resistance,  and  mechanical  strength [23 -25]. However, the hydrophilicity of the surface hydroxyl groups (OH ) results in membrane wetting, and even a small number of wetting pores may substantially increase the total mass transfer resistance, according to previous reports [26,27]. Compared with surface hydrophobic modification, regulation of the operating conditions of MC is a more economical and feasible method to avoid membrane wetting [28 -30]. In practice, the membrane state (liquid wetting and gas bubbles) can be predicted using an individual pore state model, which is mainly related to pore size and operation pressure [31]. However, how to adjust the pressure difference between gas-liquid phase is still a challenge to efficiently avoid membrane wetting, bubbling and plugging in presence of catalytic particles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Herein, particulate TiO(OH)2  catalysts were used to synergistically enhance  the  performance  of  CO2 absorption  (MEA  solvent)  and desorption with integrated membrane contact and catalytic regeneration (MC-CR) system (Fig. 1). The proposed technique achieved precise control of the operating pressure of gas and liquid flow and prevented membrane  wetting  and  gas  bubbles.  A  laboratory-scale  continuous absorption -desorption setup was designed to comprehensively evaluate the mass transfer flux, regeneration heat duty, and operational stability of the proposed technique. Specifically, the effect of TiO(OH)2  on CO2 capture efficiency and mass transfer flux was investigated at various catalytic dosages and operating conditions. The effect of solid acid on carbamate desorption kinetics and solvent thermodynamic equilibrium was  also  examined.  Certain  characterizations  were  performed  to describe the nanoparticles on the surface of the spent membrane. To verify the technical feasibility of the proposed technique, an industrialscale process was modeled for CO2 capture from coal-fired power plants.

A  laboratory-scale  continuous absorption -desorption setup was designed to comprehensively evaluate the mass transfer flux, regeneration heat duty, and operational stability of the proposed technique. Specifically, the effect of TiO(OH)2  on CO2 capture efficiency and mass transfer flux was investigated at various catalytic dosages and operating conditions. Certain  characterizations  were  performed  to describe the nanoparticles on the surface of the spent membrane. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Al-based  ceramic  membranes  were  mass-produced  by  extrusion method (Xinyuan Industry, Ningbo, China). A mixture of Al2O3 powder, plasticizers,  binders,  and  deionized  water  was  stirred  under  vacuum several times in a vacuum pulverizer and aged. The aged paste was then extruded under vacuum at a pressure of 20 -180 MPa from a mold nozzle with a predetermined shape to obtain ceramic membrane blanks. After these blanks were straightened, they were treated by drying, heating, and sintering to obtain a hollow ceramic membrane product. Pristine hollow ceramic membrane was sealed to prepare MCs. Aqueous MEA (30 wt%, analytical reagent grade, 99%) was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology and used as the CO2  solvent. Particulate TiO (OH)2 (98%, 97.88 MW, 200 nm) was purchased from Maclean and used as the catalytic material. All of the aforementioned materials were used directly without further treatment or purification.
texts.text: Al-based  ceramic  membranes  were  mass-produced  by  extrusion method (Xinyuan Industry, Ningbo, China). A mixture of Al2O3 powder, plasticizers,  binders,  and  deionized  water  was  stirred  under  vacuum several times in a vacuum pulverizer and aged. The aged paste was then extruded under vacuum at a pressure of 20 -180 MPa from a mold nozzle with a predetermined shape to obtain ceramic membrane blanks. After these blanks were straightened, they were treated by drying, heating, and sintering to obtain a hollow ceramic membrane product. Pristine hollow ceramic membrane was sealed to prepare MCs. Aqueous MEA (30 wt%, analytical reagent grade, 99%) was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology and used as the CO2  solvent. Particulate TiO (OH)2 (98%, 97.88 MW, 200 nm) was purchased from Maclean and used as the catalytic material. All of the aforementioned materials were used directly without further treatment or purification.

texts.orig: are set to counterflow mode to obtain higher gas transfer rates [32,33]. Because of the concentration and pressure gradients, the CO2 in the flue gas diffuses into the ceramic membrane tube, where it is absorbed by an absorbent and removed to achieve flue gas CO2  capture. After being passed through the ceramic membrane, the flue gas develops tiny bubbles, which enable broader and deeper gas -liquid contact and facilitate CO2  capture. The rich absorbent then enters the catalytic desorption device,  where  it  is  heated  to  regenerate,  thus  providing  continuous support for CO2  capture with low energy consumption and high efficiency in the presence of a catalyst. For more details on the experimental setup, please refer to the Supplementary Materials.

texts.orig: To  examine  the  microstructure  of  the  ceramic  membranes  and determine the effects of TiO(OH)2  on the pore size and ceramic membrane  surface,  the  pore  size,  porosity,  contact  angle,  and  scanning electron  microscopy  (SEM)  images  of  the  ceramic  membranes  were characterized before and after the experiments. To accurately determine the distribution of pore sizes on the ceramic membranes, pore size distribution was measured using a mercury intrusion method (AutoPore IV 9500,  Micromeritics  Instrument,  America)  and  bubble  point  method (PSDA-30, GaoQ, China). Porosity was then measured in distilled water as per the ISO 18754:2013 (E) standard. To examine the hydrophobicity of the ceramic membrane surface, the contact angle was measured using a contact angle measuring device (A.KRÜSS, Germany), with deionized water used as the test medium. Finally, the morphology of the inner and outer surfaces of the ceramic membranes was observed using SEM (JSM6490LV; JEOL, Japan).

texts.orig: 2.4. Process simulation
texts.text: 2.4. Process simulation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aspen  Plus  was  used  to  mathematically  model  the  energy  consumption and economics of an absorbent regeneration process catalyzed by a solid acid. To simulate the catalytic regeneration of an absorbent during CO2  capture through membrane absorption, a rigorous process model was used along with rate-based distillation, a heat exchanger, a pump, and other unit operation models in Aspen Plus. An MEA-H2O-CO2 system  was  also  used  along  with  the  ElecNRTL-RK  thermodynamic package [35,36]. As shown in Table S4, the activation energy E a  of the catalyzed MEA-H2O-CO2 reaction process was calculated using the data proposed by Li et al. [37], with other parameters set to default.

texts.orig: 3.1. Evaluation of CO2  absorption and desorption
texts.text: 3.1. Evaluation of CO2  absorption and desorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the catalytic role of TiO(OH)2 in carbamate desorption was verified in a bath reactor, with spent MEA solvent (30 wt%) at an initial loading of ~2.5 mol/L (Fig. S1). The results indicated that the addition of TiO(OH)2 substantially increased the desorption of MEA. As shown  in  Fig.  3a,  the  addition  of  2  wt%  TiO(OH)2 increased  the desorption efficiency of CO2 by 2.17 times. In MEA absorbent systems, carbamate is an amphoteric ion whose decomposition is influenced by the proton transfer process. After introduction of solid acid catalyst, the active sites on the catalyst surface decompose water to produce L- and B- acid sites, thereby creating a local acidic microenvironment, promoting the proton transfer process, and enhancing the carbamate desorption process. By facilitating the decomposition of carbamate, the powder TiO (OH)2 not only shortens the regeneration time of MEA but also lowers the regeneration temperature to allow CO2 desorption at relatively low temperatures, thereby greatly reducing the energy consumed from CO2 capture. In this study, the appropriate catalyst concentration to promote proton transfer was 2 wt% TiO(OH)2 compared to the addition of 3 wt% TiO(OH)2, since this concentration already provided sufficient numbers of active sites and reduced the risk of potential mass transfer barriers due to particle plugging during membrane absorption. Fig. 3b depicts the improvement of CO2  capture efficiency at various catalyst concentrations with fresh and recycled absorbents. When a fresh absorbent was used, the addition of 1 wt% TiO(OH)2  increased the CO2  capture efficiency by 5.39%. The catalyst concentration was further increased, the CO2 capture efficiency did not considerably increase as further increasing catalyst concentration. The Brownian motion of microparticles in the liquid promoted the mass transfer process between the gas and  liquid  films  and  reduced  the  thickness  of  the  concentration boundary layer. In addition, the irregular thermal motion of the solid particles promoted the renewal of the liquid film surface and increased the concentration difference between the gas and the liquid to increase the capacity of absorption. When a recirculating absorbent was used, the combined effects of TiO(OH)2  on membrane absorption and catalytic desorption promoted the CO2  capture effect, with a 9.48% increase in the CO2 capture efficiency with the addition of 2 wt% TiO(OH)2.

texts.orig: transfer  due  to  particle  deposition  needs  to  be  considered,  thus  the appropriate catalyst dosage for experiments in this paper was 2 wt%. The CO2  concentration in the simulated flue gas is positive with mass transfer flux, and negative with capture efficiency (Fig. 4b). As the CO2 concentration increasing to 30%, J considerably increased in a continuous manner from 27.75 to 135.03 mol (m 2 h) 1 , resulting in a 19.73% decrease in η . Moreover, when 2 wt% TiO(OH)2 was added as a catalyst, both η and J increased by 6% and 11 mol (m 2 h) 1 , respectively. Fig. 4c depicts the effects of absorbent flow on η and J . It is worth noting that when the absorbent flow rate reached 150 mL/min, most of the CO2 in the flue gas was already captured, the resistance to increase in η and J becomes greater, the tendency of η and J to increase declined. Because TiO(OH)2 contributed to membrane absorption and absorbent regeneration, comparison with a solid acid TiO(OH)2 catalyst revealed higher η and J , which increased by 4.13% and 2.73 mol (m 2 h) 1 (at 150 ml/ min), respectively. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorbent flow remained laminar, and the ability of the nanoparticles to increase the degree of perturbation during flow was rather limited, which may have deteriorated the CO2 transfer process through the addition of small deposits on the ceramic membrane surface. Under actual conditions, increasing the absorbent flow resulted in increased heat duty for desorption, indicating a tradeoff between absorption efficiency and energy consumption for desorption. Fig. 4d illustrates the effects of gas flow rate on η and J . When the gas flow rate increased from 3.5 to 8.5 L/min, a positive effect was  observed  on  the  CO2  mass  transfer  process,  and J substantially increased from 54.42 to 92.50 mol (m 2 h) 1 .  In  addition, shortening residence time for CO2 absorption resulted in a decrease in η .

texts.orig: To evaluate the reliability and performance of the proposed MC-CR system, a laboratory-scale setup was used with continuous operation for  60  min  under  various  operating  conditions  (Fig.  5a).  The  results indicated that the absorbent desorption temperature strongly affected the  capture  efficiency  of  CO2.  Therefore,  two  absorbent  desorption temperatures, namely 95 ◦ C and 100 ◦ C, were evaluated at each catalyst concentration. As shown in Fig. 5b, higher CO2 capture performance was achieved at 100 ◦ C, which was particularly detectable in the absence of a catalyst, thereby resulting in a 38% increase in ƞ . However, when the catalyst concentration increased, the high CO2  capture efficiency achieved by raising the desorption temperature decreased because the TiO (OH)2 catalyst lowered the absorbent regeneration temperature, thereby enabling increased desorption even at low temperatures. Overall, the results indicated that 2 wt% was the most appropriate catalyst concentration,  resulting  in  an  increase  of  approximately  9.48%  in ƞ at  a desorption  temperature  of  100 ◦ C  compared  with  no  catalyst  at  all. Fig. 5c depicts the CO2 desorption process at different temperatures and catalyst concentrations. According to the results, TiO(OH)2  markedly promoted the absorbent desorption process at low temperatures (95 ◦ C) from 0.08 to 0.19 mol CO2/mol MEA. texts.orig: The CO2  capture performance of the proposed ceramic membrane module was compared with that of other membrane modules in the literature in terms of four aspects: hydrophobicity, membrane material, membrane module configuration, and enhancement method (Table S5). In comparison to polymer membrane contactors in Fig. 6b~6d, ceramic membrane has obvious advantages in membrane absorption CO2  capture technology. The pore size of membrane materials is critical for CO2 mass transfer. The larger the pore size the better the CO2  transfer, but also the more likely to occur membrane wetting. Therefore, the choice of pore size has to be made in multiple considerations, neither too large nor too small. The ceramic membrane with an average pore size of 1.26 μ m used in this paper facilitates CO2  mass transfer compared to nano-pore size  membrane  materials.  Despite  being  hydrophilic,  the  ceramic membrane  used  in  this  study  operated  stably  in  unwetted  mode  by operating under a slightly negative pressure (liquid pressure 1.5 kPa lower  than  gas  pressure),  which  has  an  advantage  over  the  positive pressure operation in other literature [29,32,38,39] due to the differential pressure drive in CO2 mass transfer process, achieving high ƞ and J (Fig. 6a). Unlike other hydrophobically modified ceramic membranes, the ceramic membrane also reduced the manufacturing, operation, and maintenance costs, thereby making it suitable for industrial membrane absorption applications. As shown in Fig. 6b, the ceramic membrane exhibited  a  higher J compared with  other  membrane  materials  [34, 40 -42]. Ceramic membrane has larger inner diameter (8 mm), which facilitates sufficient flow of absorbent in the tube side and ensures that the absorbent full fills the ceramic membrane tube in experiments. For thin  hollow  fiber  polymer  membranes  (0.3 -1.4  mm),  the  flow  of absorbent in the tube may not be sufficient and easily lead to breakdowns  or  other  conditions  that  affect  CO2 transfer.  In  the  case  of different  membrane  module  configurations,  the  hollow  fiber  module achieved a higher J compared with flat sheet membrane modules [43, 44] (Fig. 6c). In addition, the ceramic membrane module outperformed polymeric membrane modules in the weaving configuration [45] and glass  bead  packaging  configuration  [46].  The  use  of  TiO(OH)2  as  a catalyst to enhance the absorption and desorption of the absorbent also resulted in higher J compared with other enhancement methods, such as the supported ionic liquid membrane method [47], nanofluid [48] and immobilized carbonic anhydrase method [49] (Fig. 6d).

texts.text: 3.3. Comparative characterization of spent membranes

texts.orig: The effect of TiO(OH)2  powder on the structure and surface (pore size and contact angle) of the ceramic membrane was experimentally characterized. As shown in Fig. 7a, the bubble point method was used to measure the pore size distribution of the ceramic membrane before and after the experiments as well as after ultrasonic cleaning for 10 min. The results  indicated  that  certain  ceramic  membrane  pores  size  were reduced after the experiments, mainly due to the reduction of some large pores  to  small  ones,  thus  resulting  in  a  more  concentrated  pore  size distribution.  However,  the  pore  size  distribution  and  porosity  of  the ceramic membrane after the ultrasonic cleaning process were almost identical to those of the fresh ceramic membrane, indicating that the TiO (OH)2  powder was only deposited on the ceramic membrane surface during the experiments and did not cause permanent blockage of the membrane  pores.  Therefore,  the  proposed  ceramic  membrane  is  expected to avoid membrane fouling through measures such as ultrasonic cleaning, which can extend its service lifespan, this is difficult to achieve with polymer membranes.

texts.orig: Fig. 7b shows the variations of water contact angle for the ceramic membrane before and after the experiments as well as after the ultrasonic cleaning process. Because the fresh ceramic membrane had OH groups with hydrophilic properties on its surface and in its pores, it exhibited a water contact angle of approximately 73.8 ◦ .  After the experiments, the water contact angle on the ceramic membrane surface reached 44.3 ◦ ,  indicating  an  increase  in  its  hydrophilicity.  However, after the ultrasonic cleaning process, the water contact angle increased to 61.1 ◦ , presumably because of the hydrophilic nature of the MEA solution used. At the end of each set of experiments, although the ceramic membrane was adequately rinsed with deionized water, some MEA still penetrated  into  the  ceramic  membrane  and  changed  its  wetting properties.

texts.orig: Compared  with  the  bubble  point  method,  the  mercury  intrusion method is more precise in evaluating medium and large pores in ceramic membranes, making it more suitable for membrane wetting analysis. In this study, the average pore size of the ceramic membrane measured using the mercury intrusion method was 1.26 μ m. Notably, the pore size of  ceramic membranes is not constant and is characterized by a lognormal  distribution,  which  may  involve  a  few  large  pore  defects (Fig. 9a). Therefore, predicting the wetting behavior of ceramic membranes on the basis of the average or maximum pore size is not feasible. Previous research [31] has indicated that a hydrophilic ceramic membrane with an average pore size of 1.26 μ m (contact angle of 51.4 ◦ ) can effectively prevent membrane wetting when 0 kPa < Δ P < 1.8 kPa, with rare  bubbles  generated  but  not  affecting  the  stable  operation  of  the system. Therefore, a gas -liquid pressure difference of 1.5 kPa (3 kPa gas pressure  and  1.5  kPa  liquid  pressure)  was  selected  for  the  ceramic membrane used in these experiments. Fig. 7b shows the state of the ceramic membrane during CO2 capture. As the operating time increased, once the ceramic membrane was wetted, visible droplets appeared on the ceramic membrane surface and dripped down to the shell side of the ceramic membrane module. Therefore, It can be seen that the ceramic membrane was maintained in an unwetted mode under a fixed differential pressure with a slightly negative pressure to achieve high η and J . Fig.  9b  shows  the  liquid  outlet  of  the  membrane  module  at  various absorbent flow rates. When the absorbent flow rate increased from 50 to 250 mL/min, the absorbent filled the pipe without bubbles close to the liquid  outlet  of  the  membrane  module,  indicating  the  complete  and stable flow of the absorbent in the ceramic membrane tube. Overally, membrane modules can be maintained in an unwetted and bubble-free state for long periods while maintaining system operation at a slightly negative pressure by adjusting the gas -liquid pressure difference.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, a practical industrial flue gas (flow rate = 120,000 Nm 3 /h,  12  vol%  CO2)  was  used  to  simulate  and  analyze  membrane absorption coupled with a solid acid -catalyzed CO2 capture process. The process primarily involved the membrane absorption of CO2  from flue gas and absorbent catalytic regeneration (Fig. 10). Initially, after denitrification,  dedusting,  desulfurization,  and  dehumidification,  the  flue gas flowed into the shell side of the CO2 capture membrane module, and the absorbent flowed in the opposite direction on the membrane tube side. The purified flue gas was then discharged into a chimney, and a rich absorbent was heated using a cross heat exchanger and allowed to flow into a vapor extraction tower to desorb CO2  and regenerate the absorbent.  The  regenerated  adsorbent  was  then  cooled  down  and repumped into the membrane modules to continue CO2 absorption. To

texts.orig: Aspen  Plus  simulation  for  CO2 capture  indicated  that  the  main operational  parameters,  such  as  the  desorption  pressure,  number  of regeneration tower plates, feed absorbent temperature, and desorption flow rate, affected the energy consumption and mass transfer coefficient K of  CO2 desorption.  A  higher  desorption  pressure  increased  the regeneration temperature to accelerate the decomposition of carbamate. However, this high desorption pressure hindered the release of CO2 from the solvent, thereby reducing the decomposition rate of carbamate. As shown in Fig. 11a, when the desorption pressure increased from 1.1 to 1.8 bar, the energy consumed decreased from 3.52 to 3.46 GJ/t CO2 and then  increased  to  3.54  GJ/t  CO2.  When  the  number  of  regeneration tower  plates  changed  from  15  to  35,  the  gas -liquid  contact  area increased,  and  the  absorbent  regeneration  efficiency  also  increased, with  additional  CO2 being  released  at  the  same  heat  duty,  thereby reducing the energy consumed from 3.51 to 3.46 GJ/t CO2 (Fig. 11b). As shown in Fig. 11c, the presence of a high feed temperature promoted absorbent regeneration, and the energy consumed decreased from 3.76 to 3.38 GJ/t CO2  with the feed temperature changing from 102 ◦ C to 108 ◦ C. As depicted in Fig. 11d, the desorption flow rate exhibited the same trend as that of the feed temperature. Under the same feed temperature and heat duty, the released CO2 increased with the desorption flow rate, resulting in a reduction in the energy consumed. When the operating parameters changed, the trend observed for K became opposite to that of the energy consumption of CO2 desorption, because K was calculated from absorbent CO2  loading at the inlet and outlet of the regeneration tower. In summary, the regeneration heat load is fixed in the simulation process, and the energy consumption of CO2 desorption is inversely proportional to the volume of CO2 desorption. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, an integrated CO2 capture system with MCs and catalytic  solvent  regeneration  was  proposed,  which  achieved  high  CO2 capture efficiency and low energy consumption through the dual facilitation of the absorption and desorption processes of a catalyst. Proper pressure regulation range of gas/liquid flow was investigated to avoid membrane wetting, bubbling and catalytic particles fouling. According to the experimental results, the energy consumed by absorbent regeneration substantially decreased by 40.8% in the presence of 2 wt% TiO (OH)2,  whereas  the  CO2  capture  efficiency  increased  by  9.48%.  The contribution of TiO(OH)2 to membrane absorption primarily manifested as enhanced perturbation of nanoparticles to improve gas -liquid mass transfer. In addition, the carbamate decomposition process promoted by catalytic desorption, thereby shortening the regeneration time of MEA or/and improving the cycle CO2 capacity. Therefore, in accordance with the experimental dynamic parameters, process simulation modeling for industrial-scale carbon capture was performed using the proposed MCCR technique, and the results indicated that the capture cost was as low as $46.7/t CO2.
texts.text: In this work, an integrated CO2 capture system with MCs and catalytic  solvent  regeneration  was  proposed,  which  achieved  high  CO2 capture efficiency and low energy consumption through the dual facilitation of the absorption and desorption processes of a catalyst. Proper pressure regulation range of gas/liquid flow was investigated to avoid membrane wetting, bubbling and catalytic particles fouling. According to the experimental results, the energy consumed by absorbent regeneration substantially decreased by 40.8% in the presence of 2 wt% TiO (OH)2,  whereas  the  CO2  capture  efficiency  increased  by  9.48%.  The contribution of TiO(OH)2 to membrane absorption primarily manifested as enhanced perturbation of nanoparticles to improve gas -liquid mass transfer. In addition, the carbamate decomposition process promoted by catalytic desorption, thereby shortening the regeneration time of MEA or/and improving the cycle CO2 capacity. Therefore, in accordance with the experimental dynamic parameters, process simulation modeling for industrial-scale carbon capture was performed using the proposed MCCR technique, and the results indicated that the capture cost was as low as $46.7/t CO2.
